text:

A hundred years ago the Olympics were also being held in Paris and a young British runner called Eric Liddell was refusing to take part in his qualifying heat, because it was being held on a Sunday and he believed in keeping holy the Sabbath Day.

His scruples were respected and his participation re-arranged. So far has regard for Christian conscience now disappeared that the French found it acceptable to open the Olympics with a ghastly parody of the Last Supper. Heaven knows what sort of scorn Liddell would face today.

We will know we have real equality when the equality laws protect whites as well as blacks, men as well as women and Christians as well as Muslims, but do not hold your breath.

That travesty of Da Vinci’s famous picture was not just crass but blasphemous. However, because that was blasphemy against Christ it is regarded with nothing more than a shrug. After all, do not people often combine His name with a profane epithet when swearing?

Now imagine if, instead of being an image of Christ, it had been an image of Mohammed. The resulting outcry would have drowned out all else and the organisers would be grovelling in apologies. Imagine if, instead of regularly invoking the Second Person of the Trinity, actors in TV dramas called upon Allah.

Father Brown notwithstanding, the average TV and film portrayal of a priest is of a paedophile, a fool or an exorcist. Try doing that with an imam.

What precisely are the rights of Christians who have been variously not allowed to display a small cross when colleagues sport hijabs or sikh bracelets, subjected to police inquisition to “check their thinking”, arrested for street preaching, denied freedom of expression, forced to affirm views their Church rejects and threatened with the sack for offering to pray for someone?

Dash it, when my friend David Amess MP lay dying the police even blocked the priest who was bringing him the last rites.

So thoroughly have Christians been marginalised that the French organisers of that horrible tableau did not even think it might be a problem. May God forgive them.

title: Paris Olympics' organisers should be utterly ashamed of anti-Christian moment | Express Comment | Comment | Express.co.uk
author: Ann Widdecombe
description: The awful parody at the Olympic Games opening ceremony was just another example of how the world views Christians as fair game, says Ann Widdecombe.
